speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Mike Bubuly came to Bovill from his birthplace in Serbia in 1913 and spent most of his working years as a lumberjack around the town. He witnessed the early life of the woods, from the fires on Beals Butte and in Bovill to fatal logging accidents and efforts to organize the men. He knew first hand some of the difficulties that immigrants might find in this new country. He describes what really happened in a fatal fight between two Yugoslavians and strongly disagrees with a rumor about the identity of the killer. Mike also speaks of the bootlegging that went on and how it involved his friend, Pat Malone. Well look, Mike, let me start asking you some more things about the old days, okay?

speaker: Michael Bubuly
words: Let's talk about that while I'm here… okay… i'll ask you some of the same things I did before, some of them.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: But one thing that I was wondering about is, do you know how your dad learned about the United States and decided to come to Idaho, how he happened to get out here? Well, he came to this country in earlier days, you know what I mean?

speaker: Michael Bubuly
words: The people went here and there because there was no industrial industry where we was at the time. And he thought he'd go somewhere where he could make some money and support the family, which was, I think it was three, it was four children and my mother. And he barred money for his parents to come over here. And he replaced, what, Minnesota, I guess, stayed in Minnesota while he worked there. And finally heard about the woods work out here in the western part of the country. So he decided to come to Idaho and come to Bovill and he worked around there for several years, you know, offered, sent some money to the family to support him back there. And finally I got big enough and I decided I wanted to come over here too. My older brother wanted to come and I wouldn't stand up for it. I wanted to come because I barely remember my dad. And I wanted to see him and I had a desire to travel and see some of the world. So he sent me many money I came.

speaker: Michael Bubuly
words: And I went to work, of course, he had a job for me, potlatch. That was my first place to work when I was a kid. And I worked with him, with the horses and all, swamping, what they called swamping, knocking limbs off the tree logs and stuff like that. And sawed logs, cross cut saw, which was mighty hard work now to tell you that. And that was in 1913 when I came here. And my dad was, he was only 56 years old then, but he was getting crippled and that was rheumatism so bad that he couldn't, he was afraid he couldn't continue working and he didn't have enough money to retire and live. So he decided he'd better leave and go back and see the rest of his family. He didn't have much money, he had about $3 ,000, all his life savings. And he went back there and he bought some land. Then a drought began to hit the Tefre and they couldn't raise the things. And they had to buy everything, they were slipping, you know, they couldn't raise anything on their own. And I think that he spent all he had and I helped him a little from here in the South Bay. And then the war broke out and he, I think he was, he was about 78 when he died.

speaker: Michael Bubuly
words: And my mother died before that. Anyway, I had two brothers and both of the Germans invaded the country, they took off as the guerrillas to fight. No, you go out and fought, keep them off. My older brother, he was out as a guerrilla fighter and they had no, they had no place to stay. They just stayed in the timber and the mountains and the rocks and the caves and all over. Finally he got pneumonia and died. And my younger brother, he was well educated. He was a guy that went to military school when World War I broke out, took him out of school, put him in the military, officer training. And he had a good education, good schooling and he was a pretty smart, intelligent guy. Anyway, when the Germans got in there, they got a hold of him some way. And he died before a firing squad. And he left two, three children. There's a picture over on the 9 o 'clock there, that's one of your daughters and my niece.

speaker: Michael Bubuly
words: And that was really pretty hard for me to take. Well, anyway, I came over here and I was probably lucky that I'd probably been in there. I'd probably been for a firing squad too. And when I came over and my dad went back and he wanted me to go with him. I told him that I didn't have money. I'd only been here a few years. And I got a little extravagant. I was spending my money. I gave him my checks for quite a while. He had probably a thousand dollars. He told me, he said, you give me your money, he said, why are you paying your own way? I said, no, I should have money besides that. He finally had to leave and I stayed. I'll tell you one thing, he was a pretty lonesome kid after he left. In St. Mary's, where he took the train, I went to have fire with him. After he left, I was a pretty lonesome knight. Because I knew I'd never see him again.

speaker: Michael Bubuly
words: But anyway, I got by pretty good. Sorry, but my house was good and I worked and made good living. Never had to go on any WPA or relief of any kind. You said it sounded like it was pretty easy to spend your money in those days. Well, there wasn't much money to spend. So I wanted a car and stuff like that, you know, and that cost money. Anyway, I was pretty fortunate. In 1934, I got jammed up pretty bad. I got my back broke. It took me a long time to get about three years before I could do anything. The doctor didn't take an airport, go back to work. But anyway, I had fused my spine, operated on it, and I got to feel fairly good, you know. So I went back to the woods again.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: How did that happen? Did it happen in the woods?

speaker: Michael Bubuly
words: Yeah, it happened in Elk River. Elk River I had a little bump up in the woods. I was top-holding the logs on the cars and loading the top man on the skipping tongs. You probably never even seen the tongs. I've seen the pictures of them. Yeah, the tongs and logs come up there and I stay up there and trip, you know, put the logs in place to change the load. And I had the heister, the guy that run the heister, he was a poor guy. He was sick, he had a cancer, and the nerves all shot. I didn't know it at the time, but he died shortly after that. He couldn't handle the logs, but he was very good, you know. And that time I was putting out, I wasn't afraid of anything. And anyway, I was on top of the load way up high and pretty rough down there. I just put the call, putting the face log on, and, you know, to build a load out, you've got to put them pretty straight. Sometimes I'd flare them right out and get more on the cars. And I was holding one log, face log, and one side I would call face log, outside log. Well, he was putting the other one on, on the other side, so I could put a chain around there. And that log, they were bringing up, got away somehow from him, and I had my head turned and caught me right across here. And over, I went about 15, 20 feet down. And I looked right on my mister log with my head, and looked right on my shoulders with my feet raised straight up. When I was knocked out, when he hit me across the back, and that giant box just like that. And I took him out, and I was bigger than my fifth, right on the center of my back there. Well, when I come to, I was on my way to hospital, on the logging train, you know, the flat car, the log logs out. And I'd say, what in the hell happened, man?

speaker: Michael Bubuly
words: So he got hurt. Boy, I tried to move, I'd say I was hurt. I couldn't budge my back, couldn't budge, couldn't move my shoulders. So I got to Al Croubert, and from Al Croubert, I had to find a transportation to Potlatch. And they got a guy there with an old car, a nurse was with me, and he got in the car, and boy, every time he made a turn, my friend, my boy, I just screamed, they kept feeding me the morphine or tablets and all those painkillers. I got to Potlatch, where I took the x-rays, and I knew the doctor well, you know, I know my dad, and doctor my dad, and stuff like that. And he told me what took place. And he told me, he said, a couple of days later, I said, I want to discourage you, but you'll never top load logs again. I said, what do you say? He said, that's what I mean. He said, I hope that it will not discourage you. I said, no. That's not a very good news, but we'll have to take it. So anyway, I was there for 12 weeks on a frame, you know, that's like that, with the laying on my back, my feet down there, my head down there. And then here, see, my stomach way up here, I couldn't turn over, couldn't, couldn't, figured that would heal that way. But anyway, 12 weeks for you, didn't, wasn't healing. So, it put me in a brace, and I wore that for 18 months. That didn't do any good. So, finally decided to have an operation. And I went for the operation, which was quite an ordeal. It took from 8 o 'clock till after almost 1 o 'clock.

speaker: Michael Bubuly
words: And I haven't been very good since on my back. It still bothers me. I still have a drainage in my back that was 1935 when the operator, still have a drainage on my back. But you went back out in the woods anyway. Yeah, I went back to the woods and worked in the woods for the last 10 years. The last 10 years I've worked in the woods, doing the same thing that I haven't done before. And didn't, oh, it wasn't as good as I was, that it's as good on my feet as I was. But I didn't mind it. I topped it out. Sometimes it's pretty tough, and sometimes it wasn't too bad. So, then I had to work to live.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Did you ever see any other guys get hurt in the woods bad?

speaker: Michael Bubuly
words: Yes, I seen guys get killed. I seen a guy that was sawed in the woods. And they saw the log drop on the side hill. And he was on the lower side? Well, anyway, they say you're blocked along, so it won't roll. When it's cut off. Anyway, when they sawed it off, the log dropped. And that prop come out, the log dropped, and rolled down. And he fell down and caught his head right between the stump, just like that. And I was off to the side of the machine in my life. He just looked like a punk when you take a punk when you squash, and he dropped something heavy on it. Blood and the brain, and everything just, oh, I'll tell you in a second. And that man never knew what happened. Just like that, just squirt, just like that, like that. And lay it there, and drop a bit of weed on it. And, oh, see, minor rags, rags never see, that's the worst one I ever seen, though. I seen the minor rags, and getting them out, you know, broke a leg, something like that. I see the guy driving a team of horses, and what they call logs that's logged together. You know, a string of logs. And he comes to a curve. This log that the horses hooked on, that it was driving, walking alongside of it. The log caught something back there, and hooked on to something, and that lifted this foot log, and drove right against his leg, yeah. Shelled this bone, clear out here.

speaker: Michael Bubuly
words: There he was, sitting there, he said, oh, God, he said, I don't know. He blended that. Before they got him to the hospital, well, he died in the hospital operating table. A young man, twenty-two or three years old. And that wasn't very pretty sight to see, either. Miles away, you know, thirty miles away from a hospital or anything. You know, that was up in a marble country, way back. It takes you, oh, it takes you a long time to get a man out. I had taken some of them out in the car. I had an automobile, and I had some of them out in there, and when they get hurt, they come get me. They come get me to get up to the hospital. I drove some of them to the hospital, and I really, really, it was a pitiful way there. Every time, a rough road, every time you had to bump or something, you know, scream. But you couldn't help it, though. We were cutting mountain roads. And I told her, I said, I just don't want to hurt you, but I said, I want to get you, I've got to get you there.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: You think that you couldn't help that there'd be bad accidents, because it was just real dangerous, or do you think that the company didn't do enough for safety, or the men didn't do enough for what?

speaker: Michael Bubuly
words: Well, it's just a pure accident. The company always cautioned the guys to be careful. They didn't want the guys hurt, because it's against their, naturally, a lot of, most of the foremen, they're human, you know, on management. And they hate to see the accidents, but the men working, sometimes more cause of it than the company, because of a little bit of carelessness. They get too confident. They think they could, like that young fella I told you to drive in that team, and if he'd have watched himself, if he'd have been on the right side of that, he would have said it wouldn't have happened. He should never have been on the inside without Loggers pulling towards him. He should have been on the other side. Something caught back there, Loggers wouldn't have did away from him. But he just happened to be a little bit careless, going on to be on the inside of that. Here where, when he caught back there, he'd have just come right over right, where they're getting his legs just crushed. So, it was just the hard luck, his own hard luck and fault.

speaker: Michael Bubuly
words: But, you know, the company always tells you to be careful. Safety first, whatever you do. I took the chances, I took the chances myself and also anybody else, you know, figure, well, it can't happen to me, but it does. I didn't think it would ever get my back broke either, but I did. I didn't think it could happen to me. I said I could get out of the way of anything. But at this time, I just happened to glance down there where in fact the log I was holding, you know, to get the chain on there, to the lazy other one on there. It was, logs come off their chute, they were all peeled and slicker, you know, and the bark knocked off and I was slicker than eel. And he just slud right off and took my, what they call, PV with it. And I looked down there just like that one. It had alone caught me cross there. And if I'd been watching, if I hadn't looked down there, that log wouldn't have gotten me because I could have died there. I'd have to log from the street and clear off the load, grab the cable, be right on the log. At that time, I just happened to look down there, thinking that the laid out log there, you know, got to have had the confidence that the wood, but it was pretty, logs were pretty slick, you know, cover off their chute. Just bark knocked on them and just sapped and everything. Just to touch anything and just slide right off. And laid one in, it was about the other one over. It slipped, the other end slipped off. I got, how long?

speaker: Michael Bubuly
words: I got crippled up, which would stay with me as long as I live. I still say that I'm pretty lucky I'm as good a shape as I am now. In my 50 years that I've been aged, I'll be 78 next week. So, I've done too bad, I think, for... But I've gone through, I think I'm pretty lucky.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: What about that fire in Bovill that they had just soon after we came? 1914?

speaker: Michael Bubuly
words: Yeah, that was my first experience on the fire, forest fire. I was working in what they call Beale's Butte, which is the area north of Bovill, there's a big ball's mouth in there. And they had a camp right on top, right on top of the Butte up there. They brought in supplies on top of the horses, up for hand-graded rolls on the field there. Zigzag up there. Anyway, that was a pretty good stand of timber up there. And they rigged up what they called Annless Line Highline. They'd take the logs from the top of the mountain, bring them down there. And at that time, that was one of the first... Outback's first experiment for something like that up there in this country. The logs, they had what they called crawlies. They'd pick up, you know, a stop there, and at the back end, them crawlies come down low to what they call Skidway. They'd put the logs in the crawlies and the sling, and nets would come down. They were about as far from here to that tree apart. And that line was a mile and a half long. And at one time, there'd probably be... probably 20 or 25 crawl roads and logs hanging right on that line from up there to down. See, they'd outload one bunch of logs down there, and that crawlie flips on the other line going back. See, and they just kept what they called merry-go-round. Empty crawlies going back, logs coming down. Anyway, they were going pretty good gate, and they were getting lots of logs out. And I think it was then... July, I think it was July, I had nothing with July, somewhere along there. A fire broke out down old Flashing in the foot of the hill, old Camp 5, and old Slashing that, in those days, they just slashed everything right on the ground. And all that stuff dry as a powder, and fire got started there, and got out of control. It started up on Mountain. Well, anyway, it was about 2 o 'clock in the morning. 2 or 3 o 'clock in the morning, they had those, what they called dinner gong. Four minutes out there pounding out of the hill, and everybody got upset. Fire, fire, fire, boys, they could hear a roar from down there someplace. And, uh, boy, we got out of there just as fast as we could. We got out of something around the ridge, away from the fire, and we got down on the other, below the fire. And you know, she seen that fire going up that mountain, got her, what they call, crown fire. I bet you could hear it five miles away. The, the roar, just like a, one of these planes way overhead. That's the way it sounded, blowing up that mountain. Right on top there, the crown fire just took everything clear up the top of it. And, uh, logs and everything, and the line, the whole thing was gone. Some, what they called the donkey, the donkey engines, and everything was gone. Even the cap and the whole thing was burned up. But, no, there was no life lost in that.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: What did you guys do that were up in the, up on the mountain working?

speaker: Michael Bubuly
words: We was cutting logs.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: I mean, how did you, what did you do when the fire came?

speaker: Michael Bubuly
words: We got out, you got out the foreman, foreman led crew out of there. Clear around on the other side, over the ridge, there's a really big ridge that runs around that basin, that Cougar Meadow Country is a big basin. Well, we had to go clear around to get up down below the fire. Because the fire was heading north, straight north. We went east, east of the east, our way didn't get around it. Until we got down below it, of course we all had to go to work and fight the fire down there. And the fire spread, fire spread. That was something.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Well, do you, what do you remember about the fighting of the fire itself?

speaker: Michael Bubuly
words: It must have been a hell of a fire to fight. It was. It finally got out of control altogether. Until it burns itself out. It came almost, I thought it was going to take Bovill too, but the wind changed at that time. Wind changed at the time and was against that. You know, wind was blowing against the fire, so that kind of checked it. That's the way they got it out of control. But the wind hadn't changed. It would have been clear, probably clear over there, the L-curver and all over. The way the wind was coming. It was clear over Bovill and over east of there. But wind changed and the luck had happened to wind change and they got to give them a chance to get out of control.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Where were you trying to fight it? Where did you try to stop it from?

speaker: Michael Bubuly
words: Right out of Bovill there, what they called Camp 8. That's just a little ways towards Clarkia. You haven't been out of it all, but you're not the crater down there. Well, I know the area, but I don't know where Camp 8 was. Well, towards Clarkia, about a mile out of Bovill, where you go towards St. Mary's. Old there, Bear Hills back there. That was all timber. That's where a fire was stopped. It's all that flat there, that Cougar-Minter country, what they call Cougar-Minter country. That was all burnt. That big butte back there, clear over towards Clarkia, you know, where you go over. Divide there, clear over to that.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Did you have anything to do with when they, did you see when they were trying to get, when people were getting out of the town, evacuating the town?

speaker: Michael Bubuly
words: Well, they were getting out of town before we got there. When we got to Camp 8, and a company had a logging train there, you know, bringing the people out, bringing the men out. We got there, and Milwaukee railroad bridge was a fire there. Just across a little creek, it wasn't probably 30 feet long there, it was 40. But it was fire under it and burning, and it was afraid to go across it. Of course, most of us could have walked out and walked the road and walked down, but they were all on that train, about five or six hundred men. And this, one of the engineers said, well, my family's in Bovill, so I've got across there. So he took his engine and went across. But he was resting in their palms. He got across there, so they figured it was safe for them. But anyway, it was, most of the people was gone out of Bovill. They were having a potlatch and all that way, and all that. The train took them down there. And they were getting ready, everybody was getting ready to leave.

speaker: Michael Bubuly
words: And that's an experience that I probably don't very easy forget to see when a blaze like that, how fast it could travel. It's amazing. It traveled, I wouldn't say it down here, that a fire traveled 70 miles an hour coming up the bridge there, but no fire traveled that fast. But it traveled fast enough that you'd have quite a time keeping ahead of it if you were trying to get away from it.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: On foot or in a car?

speaker: Michael Bubuly
words: A foot. A foot. But they got me trying, I've heard them tell down here, this big old gloss down here, whatever you call it. The fire was traveling 70 miles an hour. The fire can't travel, it won't travel that fast. Wind don't blow that fast.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: So if we get 70 mile wind, it wouldn't be much left in front of it, would it?

speaker: Michael Bubuly
words: So I don't think that the fire, the exaggerator of it, but I'll say that it'll travel 10 miles an hour. It gets the right bimp and a little uphill. Boy, she crowns pretty fast because that heat would have had a bit of an effort and it just melts.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Dude, the town of Bovill itself, wasn't there a fire there about the same time as that in the town?

speaker: Michael Bubuly
words: Well, that's where the town burned at.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Were you there when that happened?

speaker: Michael Bubuly
words: Yes, I was there that night. 4th of July. I was in town. I was in town for the 4th of July celebration. They got us out of bed. The building that I was in didn't burn, it stopped at the highway going through there. The guy that owned the building there, he got everybody up in the town, it's a fire, the town is burning. All we could do was get out there watching burn. There was nothing that you could do. She was pretty hot firing there too. People, everybody in town was around there watching the burn and nobody could do anything about it because the water pressure wasn't good enough. Firefighting equipment was absolutely not practically not. There was some that lived in the mountains. The bank burned up there, and the hotels and restaurants and post office and the whole thing went there. It was all framed buildings. There was no brick building there. A lot of guys were nervous that the money burned up in the bank and everything burned up. Nobody lost anything in the bank at that time because they had everything in the safe. The next day, I was around there and they asked me if I wanted to work. I said, hell, I'll just get it. I said, hell, yes, there's no extra money. So I worked to help uncover the safe, you know, and bought coal and stuff, you know, uncover the safe. Nothing in the safe was burned up, but everything else was level with the town. All the hotels and beds and frames and all that was twisted from the heat.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Did you have any idea how that one, that fire started?

speaker: Michael Bubuly
words: Well, I figure the one guy, there was a guy that, a couple of guys, had some money in the bank. And I understood that one of them drew $700 or something like that on the bank the day before. That day, that same day, the fire, that night, the fire started. And them guys, I think two of them burned up in the fire. And there was room where somebody could have went in there and robbed them and perhaps killed them and set a fire. And that was possible, but there was nothing ever, definitely nothing that had been proved on anybody or proved that that's what happened. But there was pretty strong suspicion that something like that had happened. Because them two guys were the only ones that burned up. This guy had to do money helping these partners. So something like that could have happened. Of course, those days, it was very rare for something like that to happen, but still could have happened.

speaker: Michael Bubuly
words: And finally, after all, the town rebuilt. And it was pretty good for a while. I don't know how it is now. But I imagine she's pretty well run out of this town part of it.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: It rebuilt fast after that?

speaker: Michael Bubuly
words: Yeah… it rebuilt right after the fire. It started rebuilding. All that block, brick building, there was no frame building there.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: What did you think when you first came over and you first came to Bovill?

speaker: Michael Bubuly
words: It sounds like it would be pretty wild and wooly country for a young kid. Well, it was kind of strange. Language was the biggest barrier. The people was all right, but I couldn't understand what they were saying, what they were talking about. You know, it's quite a feeling when somebody's talking to you and you don't know what they mean, what their meaning is. I look at them, just as I said, I don't know, I can't understand you, but a lot of them, you know, they talk to you. Oh, it's kind of a strange feeling. You can imagine yourself now going into Russia or someplace and they can talk to you in Russia. You know they're talking to you, but they don't know what the hell they're saying. But it didn't take me long to catch on. I began to, one of my dad was pretty good, he didn't speak to a plane, but he understood better than he could speak it. And he began to teach me right away. Well, I learned a month or two while ago, I could understand a hell of a lot what they're saying. It's easier to understand than it is to speak. It's easier to understand the words than it is to speak them the pronouncil. But a year or two I was around with young people and having, chasing around, getting a great number of girls. I got along pretty good.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Was there a pretty good social life there when you were growing up there, a teenager?

speaker: Michael Bubuly
words: Yes, I think it was. I really think it was better than it is today. The people were a little familiar. That is, you know, you get acquainted each year. I didn't have no trouble at all getting acquainted with the people, and people accepted me as one of them, and I felt as one of them. And got along with them good. A lot of, you know, first few years where I perhaps didn't understand everything they were saying, but anyway, I'd figure out what they were saying later, you know. I got started writing, reading. Well, like you're saying about the girls, that's something that I've wondered about. I figured that there probably couldn't have been very many girls with all the lumberjacks around.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: But for the young guys, it wasn't too hard to get dates and stuff like that?

speaker: Michael Bubuly
words: Oh, yeah.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: I mean, they were with the town girls, huh?

speaker: Michael Bubuly
words: Yeah, sure… you know, you get them off the young people those days, like in the holidays. There were a lot of girls in order to see someone. Well, I like to get acquainted with them, and you'd see a girl, well, I'm going to get acquainted with them, and eventually you'd just get together. I just figured there'd be a lot of lumberjacks, and there wouldn't be very many towns. Well, most of the lumberjacks were older people. There wasn't too many young lumberjacks in those days. Mostly older people. There was, occasionally they'd find two, three young brothers in the camp. That is to be, you know, in their thirties and forties. And nowadays there's more young people in there, which is because they're making more money nowadays. The more young lumberjacks today, of course, today they wouldn't hire an old lumberjack. It's got to be young men who they hire. In those days, they hired older ones, experienced.

speaker: Michael Bubuly
words: And, of course, every town, there wasn't too many, too many girls in Bovill, that is, because town only, it was only about 500 at that time. But it wasn't too hard to get acquainted. There's one thing that I never did take to was dancing, and I couldn't, never learned to dance. But I'd go to dance, but I wouldn't dance. I'd go there and I'd sit around, talk with people. To dance, I never, I don't know, just never got taken fancy to it. And that kind of holds the guy back. If you get dancing and you get acquainted quicker.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Do they have much parties and stuff like that, too?

speaker: Michael Bubuly
words: Oh, yes. Yeah. Gatherings… in later years, all the gatherings, parties,… changed a little bit. It was more to drinking, more drinking parties. But in the first days I was around there, there wasn't too much of that going on. Young people didn't take to these wild drinking parties until later in the 20s. But you take, in between four 20s there, there wasn't too many of those, there wasn't too many people, young people drinking. But in the after 20s there, I made up for it. Moonshine comes. I wonder why that was. Nowadays you think of young people drink, that's the way it is. Well, it's just, I don't know, just to be doing something, I guess. To find a recreation, I guess, or something that... I was thinking why they didn't drink before, back in the 20s. Well, I tell you, there wasn't, I don't think there was so much drinking among the older people in those days either. I think what the younger people learned is right at home. Most of them. I think the examples set for them and I think that followed.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: What about the old lumberjacks? What kind of guys were they like, the guys that would be the lumberjacks in the camps, when you were young and the old timers that were in those camps?

speaker: Michael Bubuly
words: Well, there's only one thing that I found out when I was a kid. Some of the old lumberjacks, they didn't like to out-porn people. I know one guy in particular, a boy every time, luckily, he's just the greatest thing to do. Why did you come here? And I thought I was kind of, I don't know, if he'd only stop or think, some of his ancestors came just like I did, but some of them don't see it that way. As Tom, the guy the other day, called me on the telephone and he says to me, he said, I'm not poring her like you are. I said, oh, I said, you're just not smart enough to realize that you are. No, I'm not. I didn't come from over there. I said, you probably didn't, but I said, where the hell did your ancestors come? Oh, it probably came from England someplace but I was never there. I said, well, what do you figure that you're more American than anybody else?

speaker: Michael Bubuly
words: And as I said about this, some of them, oh, one guy in particular, he didn't like me. He just thought I was going to take his job or do something. I don't know what that's, that's hurting. And he didn't like me, I know, because he just, but a lot of them are just as nice to me as my dad was, you know, toward me.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Were a lot of the bachelors?

speaker: Michael Bubuly
words: Mostly bachelors. Mostly bachelors. Probably 50%. It's a gold one. Come from someplace from here to go from there to there… and that's the way it,

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: by the way, you read that book, did your trees grow tall?

speaker: Michael Bubuly
words: Yeah. Yeah, I wish I could get a hold of that guy that wrote that book. I'd correct him on something there. When he said that the old Sam P. Wash was the guy that murdered the guy in Elk River in 1912 or 1914. He was just a little bit of a man there. Well, he was just all wrong because old Sam P. Wash never was in Elk River until almost in the early 20s. Never been to Elk River. He lived in Bovill, but he just never, never went to Elk River. And I'd like to see that guy sometime. I might see him in Alaskan word and I highly got his information.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Did you know Sam Piwash?

speaker: Michael Bubuly
words: He was a very good friend of mine. A friend of my dad's. They were partners for years. You know, they're batched together and had their homes together and everything. And my dad had a reputation, one of the honest guys, one of the very best integrity and honesty that anybody could have. He never had a bad word for anybody or would do harm to anybody. He's a real honest man and old Sam was his best friend and they were together. In fact, when this murder took place, I don't worry, it took place. I never was up there before that or quite a while after that. But I know just exactly where it took place. And a guy that did it, he was never caught. He got out of there and he's gone. Nobody ever saw him ever since. By the way, he got out of there. He rode the train out and nobody knew him. With a cast on his leg.

speaker: Michael Bubuly
words: After he got out of there, that cast was off and he was gone. But anyway, the murder was forced onto him. This bully guy, it was a public place where it happened, wild house. And this guy was a big bruiser, twice as big as the other guy and he was abusing it or beating him up. Kicking him and everything and guy got tired of it and he just took a knife and ripped him open and got out. And the only way to get out of Elk River is this guy's himself and he cast on his leg and put him on a train where he went. And where he went from there, God knows, and never did find him. But anyway, this guy said it was Sam. He wasn't in it, but Sam did not do it.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: This bully that got killed, had he been around there for very long? Did he have a reputation for being a bully?

speaker: Michael Bubuly
words: No… the guy that got killed? Oh yeah, he was awful abusive. Abusive because he was big and powerful. See, he just thought there was nobody as big as or as strong as he is and little liquor when he was very abusive. And I know a couple of answers like another one happened on a fern one in the same manner. Only happened on a job. Or a big guy, he was kind of over shear. He was a bully and he got taken on a little bit of a guy. A little guy weighed about 150-120 pounds. And slapping him and stuff like that and a guy got tired of that. So he took a gun and shot him, killed him. He killed him and it all depends. But anyway, the guy went through the pen attention and he died. Died there. Nowadays he's out. So that's the way the Al Curban said it happened. The guy took it as long as he could and the guy wouldn't leave him alone. He kept following him around. So he just thought, well I'll have to do something. You can't tell him otherwise. He just stuck a knife through him and spilled something and went on about his business.

speaker: Michael Bubuly
words: So there were quite a few people saw that because it was in a public place there. They saw it happen. They know who did it. They know who did it. But they never could find him.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Did the people not want to tell at the time?

speaker: Michael Bubuly
words: They wanted this guy to get away because it wasn't his fault. Well some of them didn't tell him because it wasn't his fault. Most of the people wanted him to get away because the guy was abusing him. Man, so much stronger than you are. You can't just sit there and let him... Beat you up. Beat the hell out of you. Just because he's bigger. So these people know that Fred is the one that fixed him up in the cast to put him on the train. Figured out the way to get out. He got out that way. He got out right on the train. Never did catch up with him. Where he went I don't know. God knows where he went.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Was there a law man there or a cop in Elk River at the time I wonder?

speaker: Michael Bubuly
words: Well they usually have a deputy or something down there, a town. That was outside of the city limit, outside of town. That was in the county, but the town county was around... The guy was gone and the better guy was dead. There was no court or trial over that or not because they couldn't find the guy. I thought he was gone. They just couldn't figure out where he went.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Well what about Sam Piwash?

speaker: Michael Bubuly
words: I've heard of him before. I've heard that he had a real successful business through the years. He did good in the area. He kept going and gave a lot of people jobs. During the Depression he was the only one that was hiring around there. He had this little dinky logging business. He kept a lot of guys from going hungry. He was making fence posts and cutting pulp wood and saw logs and stuff like that. Getting guys, you know, a lot of family guys around there. That's the only thing they could do. They could find or do. Sam had something for them. Didn't pay much, couldn't pay much because he wasn't getting very much for his stuff. But anyway, they made a living. He was good for the town. He helped out and supported quite a few in that town during the Depression. He didn't make any money himself. He had the pleasure of hiring somebody and giving them a chance to work. He didn't become rich or anything like that.

speaker: Michael Bubuly
words: He was a good hearted man. He'd help anybody that was in need. And he did help a lot of them. He was liked, but someone disliked by something. Some of them didn't like him because he figured he was making money or doing good for himself. But he really didn't make any money. He never... Oh, is that enough? If I want a bar, $400, $500, just my plain note. Nothing else. He got wishmated back. And he always told me, like, if you need anything, let me know. Yeah, he's that kind of a guy. He's just a great... He's the guy that built that business in that bank. He's the guy that made that bank what it is. That bank has got to work something like $25 million deposit there in the city, a town of 600, 600, 700 population. Yeah, that's a big bank over a big area. Yeah. Frank Brocke's the one that built it. I knew Ollie Bowman, that was a part owner there, that owned probably half of a statement green. And I knew Willis Bowman. You probably know him. He's a very nice guy. I like Willis.

speaker: Michael Bubuly
words: But this guy that's in there now, that's the president of the bank, I don't recall him. Newton, isn't it? Was he in there with Brocke was in there?

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: I don't know how long he was there. He was there for...

speaker: Michael Bubuly
words: What kind of a look at Guy is he?

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: He's bald… tall? Quite tall? Bald?

speaker: Michael Bubuly
words: Not too tall. I guess he's...

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Kind of dark and bald or...?

speaker: Michael Bubuly
words: Yeah. He's got glasses too. I know how he is because he was in there before because he was sitting there and inside there working all the time at the desk. Yeah, he's been there for some years… then... Is that..… whoa. Lindstrom. Not Lundstrom… oh darn it.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Lundstrom?

speaker: Michael Bubuly
words: Sunstrom. He's there. Yeah. Yeah. Sunstrom, yeah. He's been there quite a while too. Ray.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Ray, isn't it?

speaker: Michael Bubuly
words: Yeah. I know his dad, Guella. But he always... Well, I'm going to ask you about Pat Malone.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Pat?

speaker: Michael Bubuly
words: Oh, Pat is a very good old guy. Pat was quite a humorous guy. He was a deputy there and he had a little austerity. And of course, he had to show a little bit for... do something for his money that he was getting, whatever little he was getting. And every once in a while he had to get on to somebody and call him out or tell him what he was going to do to him. Like it was south. He thought I was bringing whiskey into town and he told me, he said, I'll get you. I said, okay, Pat, go ahead. He said, anytime you want to stop me, you stop me. I said, I'll never resist. He said, I'm willing to let you search me or car anytime. I'll get you, he said. They told me that you're bringing whiskey into town. I said, yeah, right in here. Oh, he said, I was bringing whiskey. They told me, I said, they'll tell you a lot of stuff, Pat. I said, I'm not. Well, be careful, they said. I'm laying for you. I said, thank you for warning me. But he was really a good old guy. He had dinner with us several times, you know, wife and me. Yeah, just five days before he died. Yeah, just shortly before he died. He had beer. He liked beer, he liked whiskey. But he told me, he said, I'll tell you how I drank with you people. But he said, I don't do it with everybody. I said, Pat, you're just the same. I haven't drank here. He said, don't worry, nobody's lost. And he had a trust. He trusted us.

speaker: Michael Bubuly
words: Will you tell me that story about how first they stopped you out on the road and then in town you had some stuff. That was such a good story. I really liked that one. That was one where Pat was, because it's happened to other guys too. You said you were on your way back from Spokane or someplace. Oh, when Pat and the Sheriff stopped me? Yeah, I was coming back from Spokane. That's before I was married. I wasn't even married then. I was getting around quite a lot, you know, from the Spokane here and there. Of course, I always liked a little nip of whiskey and moonshine. I never carried the excess, but I always liked to have a drink occasionally. Anyway, I had that big touring car. Great big thing. Stevens. They don't really build them anymore. But anyway, I was coming from Spokane, I come out of Derry, an old road. Went right straight ahead and was swinging this way over that hill there. What they called Benson Hills, down the last week I got Derry. Derry, that road was way down around that way, and that what they called Benson Hill went around that point. It was a curve, just like an uphill and a curve. I come down through there, there was a car right straight across the road.

speaker: Michael Bubuly
words: So what in the hell is going on here… we got you… i said, what… we got you this time. I said, what the hell you got me for? What are you talking about? I said, you got me blocked there, I know. I said, where's the whiskey? I said, right here. Oh, you've got some here. I said, no, I haven't. And I had a little bottle like that. I just drank it before, after I left Troy. And threw the bottle out. Then I said, well, go ahead. I got out, you know. I said, get out. So I got out. What do you carry it… i said, Jesus… why ask me… i said, there's the car. Look. Look through there, we'll get you yet.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Was this Pat, too?

speaker: Michael Bubuly
words: Yeah, Pat was there, yeah. He didn't say, but the summer feels that I'll be watching by you. I said, well, thank you. Nobody's got to watch for me. So, Pat told me after it, he said, Mike, I said, we had a report that you were coming through, there was a load of whiskey. He said, Charlie, Charlie Sunkerville, he said, call me up, and he said, come up and get me. So, he said, we didn't. Actually, you didn't get anything, Pat, did you? No, he said, we didn't. I was glad we didn't, to say. I was glad we didn't. Another time I came. I did have, that time I really did have whiskey. I had five gallons in the back seat in the same car, blanket hanging over the front seat, and right in the back seat, touring car. Back seat and blanket over it that way. I stopped right in front of the hotel. The old Pat seen me and he come out. He said, you know, tell me you were running whiskey, so you're bringing whiskey into this town, and he said, I'm going to get you. I said, Pat, you're welcome to stop me any time you want to. I said, any time you want to stop me, stop me. I said, I'll stop and you'll go, search. He said, I will do it. I said, okay. I said, Pat, I'm hungry, I'm going to eat. It's a bad problem here, right here. He said, long time ago, I was eating. I was talking to me. I said, if I have gallons of moonshine in the back seat of that car right there in front. Well, he didn't think it'd be foolish enough to bring the whiskey in there. I wasn't selling it. I just abided for myself and my chums.

speaker: Michael Bubuly
words: They'll pay for so much and I'll pay for so much, and we'll all have moonshine. Pat, I had the good notion to tell him after that, but I don't think I ever told him. I'll tell you, I bet you the laughter himself I did. Him knows right over it. They were talking to me in the five-gallon keg in there.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: How did you happen to have five gallons in there in town at once? You didn't often take five gallons through Bovill, did you? How did you happen to have five gallons in the back at once?

speaker: Michael Bubuly
words: Five-gallon keg. Five-gallon keg. Wouldn't keg, you know. I bothered to, you know, work with the guys and say, well, if you have a round or good bargain on the whiskey, I'll take a gallon. The other guy'd take a gallon, so I'd pay so much for it, and they'd pay me, and I have my own, and they have their own. And I was taking it for them guys, you know. We'd divide it up. We'd divide and pay equal amount and divide the whiskey.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Was that one that you got a real good deal on, on that five gallons?

speaker: Michael Bubuly
words: You told me. I thought you said somebody you got, this guy had to get rid of it. Yes. In fact, I got it. It was telling you for 15, between 15 and 20 dollars. So this guy said, Mike said, I got B out of 10 gallons. He said, Pat is after me. He said, on my trail, he said, would you take this off my hand? I said, geez, how would I do it? I said, I can't take all of it. So, I said, I'll take five gallons. He said, I'll take $50 for five gallons. I think he was selling for $18 or $20 a gallon. He said, I want to get rid of it. I said, okay, I'll take five gallons, so I give him $50. He said, what in the hell am I going to do with this other five? I said, well, I don't know. I said, I have no more money. I said, I'll give you all the money I had. You know, I didn't have any more. I had $50 and I give it all. I probably had $1 or $2. I really said, I'll tell you, take the damn stuff and hide it someplace.

speaker: Michael Bubuly
words: So, I take it and hide it in the woods just a mile and a half on a boat wheel there. I went back to camp and I had this other, you know, guys that wanted me to buy them some. I got that stuff to them and they paid me their share. I went back to camp and I was, oh, about two, three weeks. I came back and I came back and it all didn't come through for about a month. Here they had a railroad right away, cut through there where I had the damn whiskey hit. I said to myself, why didn't I have that one open? It was gone. They cut right through there where I had it hit. And the fellow named Frank Mallory was a right away foreman, you know, a railroad grading foreman. And he told me, he said, one day he said, you know, he says, a funny thing happened. He said, I took a crew out there and he said, told them what to do and put them, told them what I want. And I left. I came back and said, everyone broke our dentists.

speaker: Michael Bubuly
words: I said, how can that be? He said, I don't know. I said, I know. He said, what do you mean? I said, I had some whiskey hit in there. I said, they ran around and drank it all. He said, was it your, did you hide it there? I said, yes, I did. I said, were you going to pay for it? He said, no, I said, I don't know. I wouldn't need it. I said, it didn't cost me anything either. Anyway, I told him in the right, that was Leslie Mallory. He said, my God, I couldn't figure out how in the hell he said. Someone in which they always had a gun drunk because you couldn't budge them while they got into it. Anyway, that guy, that bootlegger come next time I saw him. I said, well, I said, you're a take of whiskey. I said, I hid. You better collect from Leslie Mallory for it. I said, what do you mean? I said, his crew found it and got drunk. I said, you couldn't work for two days.

speaker: Michael Bubuly
words: You can figure out how his men got drunk on the job, because they all carried out little lunch. Leslie, you know they didn't take any booze in there. Well, anyway, they found a way to get it. They had this, you know, lunch, thermos bottles and stuff, you know, they just kept filling them. And they did it. I just, I don't know whether they drank all five gallons or whether somebody stashed some of it away. But anyway, 25, 30 men, you know, they were all drunk in hell. They lay down, fallen all over the south, nobody working. And that was my fault because I cast it in the wrong place. And it just never ripped that they were going to cut the roadway through there. I know they were going to put a road, a railroad through there sometimes, but I didn't know they were going to cut it right, go right through there when I put the damn cane.

speaker: Michael Bubuly
words: Well did most of the moonshine that was drunk around there come from around Bovill or did it come from, it was brought in from Spokane or further, you know, far away. Most of it comes from Orofino. know. Most of it comes from a soccer, around a soccer, there were people on the soccer in a Greer country. Some come from the Spokane, most of it comes from up there because there's one guy that he had a pretty big outfit, a fellow named Schultz. And he'd always, he'd always look me up to see if there's any, he'd give him a hint where he could do some business. Of course, he always get me a bottle or two. That'd give him a hint to be somebody wants to buy a, you don't mind if I smoke. I'll give him a hint if somebody wants to buy a gallon or so, you know, a lot of good advice. You know, even family, but he's a partner, buy a gallon of it and have it home.

speaker: Michael Bubuly
words: It's not wrong with that as long a man didn't go to his dreams. I didn't know that. Oh, okay, thanks, my dad.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Can I have one of those two?

speaker: Michael Bubuly
words: I enjoy cigarette also. You want a cigarette? Yeah. Well, I didn't, he didn't think that you'd smoke. No, I don't usually, but I, you're welcome to it. I talk to, you know, a lot of people, a lot of people don't like to be around somebody smoking. That's why I ask you to smoke. Well, I like to smoke once in a while myself. I quit for several years and a while back, I don't know, it seemed like a pass of time wave or something. Because I, you're busy all the time, but I'm not. I got sat around and get out walking and when I'm walking I like to light a cigarette and let the ashes fall where they were. Oh, anyway, life is one big experience. You learn to live, live to learn and a lot of things happen, interesting things. Some things that's not too interesting, but anyway, you look back after you get to be up in the years like I am, you can look back and say, well, a lot of those things I enjoyed. I just kind of, I enjoyed them anyway, like working hard and having close calls and accidents and stuff like that.

speaker: Michael Bubuly
words: And all in all, there's a lot of pleasant experiences, a lot of bad ones too. In the course of 50 years, 60 years, 50 years, I have a slave market. You learn a lot of things. Just one thing is wrong with you, you don't learn enough to come out as a wealthy man or something. Well, probably better off that way, probably better off being honest. I believe right today, I think I'm happier, a lot of people has got a lot of money. I know that I have not hurt anybody or robbed anybody, killed anybody. I know that my mind is clear, my conscience is clear. And I think that's something that really works well, I think. I always paid my bills, my credit has always been good and still is good. And I never, I wouldn't say that I walk for anything today, I'm living good. I hope I can say the same thing when I'm your age, that I never hurt anybody. I hope you can't do it, that's something that's really worthwhile, I think.

speaker: Michael Bubuly
words: I always figured, man, if you got, I don't care how much you got, it's yours. And what I got, I figure it's mine. If I need anything, I'll come over and ask you for it. And if I got to come over and ask me, if we get along, we got to do that, if we'd all do that. This will be better for the world to live in, instead of going to say I'll take it… because what's yours… if you got a dollar, if you got a thousand dollars, if you got ten thousand dollars or twenty thousand or more, it's yours. I didn't work for it, I didn't make any cures. And same way applies to me, same thing applies to me. And if I need or something, if I need something, if I know you, I'll say, well, Fred, I need something if you could help me out. If you say yes, all right, if you say no, all right. And I feel the same way the people come to me. If I had it, I was in possession to help somebody, if I can, which perhaps in my days I did. And I don't think that I lost too much.

speaker: Michael Bubuly
words: Perhaps lost a few dollars in there, but what the hell? Because the difference. A few dollars didn't hurt me, probably helped them so.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Is there any time you're thinking in particular when a guy needed help in times in the old days when you had money and other guys didn't?

speaker: Michael Bubuly
words: Well, not really any emergency. It probably come at the time that somebody asked me for something that they probably didn't need. I'd say, no, he doesn't work for a purpose. You know, like, the guy wants a bar or something to go and have a big drunk or something like that. I don't think that's necessary. But if that's a family or something, run short of something, if I have it out gladly, split my life. But otherwise, I never had no any too much experience with somebody, you know. I always learned, like, Frank Brocke, I think, is the same way he'd really help a guy out if you thought he needed it. Frank, I think, is one man that I know, one that I know that would do things like that for people that know that all they got is what they earned. But Frank is pretty honest man himself and he trusted honesty. I want to prank time or two that I really had to have some money. I said, Frank, I'll give you a minute. Morbue guy at the house. No, Mike, you're awarded to the Morbue. So, $500, I want the size 500, that's a lot of money, you know, to loan somebody just to warranty the living. And, uh, Frank has never turned me down. And I know my, and when this old Sam Pibwatch died, he had the guy that was with him all the time there. And the old Sam was honest and he thought the old guy should, that he should take care of the old guy. Well, he wasn't well enough exponential to do that. And before he died, I was here in Spokane, I got a call from Pibwatch, from my doctor.

speaker: Michael Bubuly
words: He said, uh, your friend Sam is, if you want to see him, you better come down. He said, because he's not going to be around too much longer, you get a heart trouble. And, uh, I went down that night, had a track come from work. And that was all right, back up, you know, back it up. And old Sam, he, he wanted me to, uh, can't take over his business where he left off. And I told him, I said, no Sam, I can't do that. But at the same time, I, I got, I got sick and I was off work for quite some time. And, uh, anyway, I told, I told Rocky, I said, uh, what Sam said, well, he said, we'll get out and I'll talk to Sam. And he owed the bank quite a lot of money, something like $8 ,000. So I told Frank, I said, it's, uh, as far as I'm concerned, I know I'm not, I'm not there. There's enough there for me to even bother with it. But I said, I will kind of look after things till, uh, after he passed away, I told him, I said, we'll look after things and, and, uh, you'll perhaps get your money back on him. But anyway, after Zoll settled, it was about $4 ,500 left. And Frank, he said, we got to put a limit to this. He said, you can't leave him in your care forever. He said, you've got to limit this limit to amount of money that's in there. He said, after that money is gone, he said, you'd have to keep them if you don't. So he made up a will that way, see. And the old man thought it was all right. And that's the way it went. And I kept the old guy, you know, kept him with the money from the estate. He was all gone. Of course, I got my, I got my, uh, administrator's fee out of it. I had to, I had to have something out of it. And the old guy, after money was gone, I, I couldn't, I couldn't keep him. I had no, of course, there's no relation to me or anything.

speaker: Michael Bubuly
words: So I just told the attorney that, what's what? Well, he said, all we could do is to put him in a home. I said, well, I haven't got, I said, I can't keep him because I've got myself taken out, my wife and my home. I said, I, I just can't possibly do anything for him. Well, he said, well, I'll just have him put him in a home. I said, well, put him in a home and they passed away there. So I didn't get very much. I didn't hardly get paid for my work today. My time that I spent up from there, traveling back and forth from here to there. All those years that I took care of it, I got $900 on a whole thing. And I know I spent more than that, and some people thought that I was, that I inherited a lot of money. I said, you should have written a letter. Everything was on the paper, everybody owed. And the bills that he run here and there, he had the cattle, he had almost 100 had the cattle, but the cattle was worth about seven cents a pound.

speaker: Michael Bubuly
words: So Frank, he arranged and sold the whole thing. And I put that, left that in a bank there to, to take care of the old guy.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Did Sam come from the same part of Europe that your father came from?

speaker: Michael Bubuly
words: Yes. Yes. Not from the same, exactly same town or territory, but from the same, same province.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Were there, were there a lot of guys out there that came from Yugoslavia in that area? Were there a lot of guys around Bovill?

speaker: Michael Bubuly
words: There was at that time, yeah, there was at that time in early, I mean, ten, nineteen-eight before I came. And when I came there, there was quite a few of them. There was, I'll say probably a couple hundred working around there. There wasn't, you know, they didn't have the homes or they would just love it, actually. When they're not working there in Spokane or having that little shack in town someplace, Troy or Derry or Bovill or something, you know, shabby. When they're not working, they're back, say they do their own cooking and stuff like that. If work opens up again, they'll go to work.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Do you know if a lot of these guys started on the extra gangs or did they start as lumberjacks and ride?

speaker: Michael Bubuly
words: Well, most of them was lumberjacks. Of course, some of them worked on what they call section work on railroads and repair and upkeep and stuff like that. But most of them was lumberjacks. Sawyers, sawyers or sawing or decking or something like that, you know, skidding. They're more of a sawyer than anything else. The saw logs with crosscut saw, not these power saws now, crosscut.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Was there timber in Yugoslavia too or had they had lumberjack experience before they came over?

speaker: Michael Bubuly
words: Yes. There was quite a bit of forest. The part where I came from, there was quite a forest industry, not too far from there. And a lot of them worked there. But most of them came, that come once, that came to the Idaho and Washington, Oregon. So we have mostly Idaho. They were raised right in the timber in a natural, you know, outing that they just loved to be in the timber, to be in the wilderness. And they enjoyed it. And that's why most of them come out this way. A lot of them like mining and stuff like that. They want to mining, but most of them come out here just for that reason because they love to be in the timber and trees and stuff like that. And that was the heaven up there around Marble Creek and all throughout the bow-wheel there. And now coming through there and they come to a spring coming right out of there, rocks. And in July and August when it's so haunted in their shade, that water ice cold, you know, when you get down there and back and say, it's so cold it hurts your teeth. Well, that was pure water. That wasn't polluted. And I think that's something that's very important to end life and something that's pure like that. And now most of them enjoyed being out in there all the time.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Were there many guys young like you or most of the guys older?

speaker: Michael Bubuly
words: Well, most of them, it was mostly older guys. It was pure young ones that came here, came down there. You know, their brother would be here or somebody and their uncle or dad would send for them when they come. But towards the last, now you take around 20, some of them younger guys that was lumberjacks and they got around around the cities of Spokane and Seattle and stuff like that. And they seen the bootlegging and that stuff there. A lot of them went into that. Instead of working for hell, I could make easier money. I would go to moonshine or run moonshine for somebody or something, you know, which I was offered that job time and time again. I had a big car and a big guy played here in town. And I went to Hawaii to stay in the woods. Why don't you get downtown here and live like a man? I said, I'm living like a man where I'm at. I said, it's living better than you are.

speaker: Michael Bubuly
words: Who the hell are you? You're not making money as you can make here. The bad guys offered me $200, $250 a week to run whiskey from Madelaine Falls here. I said, no, no. No, no. If you go to jail, we'll pay you $10 a day when you're in jail. I said, no, I don't want to go to jail. But a lot of them, you know, fall for that. A lot of them young guys want to. Oh, they did good. But after all, they come out broke. No better than I had. I didn't work in the woods. Someone who got in trouble was deported because there wasn't citizens. They were deported. They couldn't get in trouble so many times that they got tired of them. But they took them out of the country. If that state had worked like they should, it would have been just better off. You know, you get in there, something like that, you make money outright. But you don't hang on to that money. The more you make more, you feel free that you could spend. And the first thing you know, you're buying automobiles. That's way beyond your means. You're taking a lot of trips and traveling that's way beyond your means. And you get into things that you really shouldn't.

speaker: Michael Bubuly
words: Of all the big shots, bootleggers around here, everyone that I know went broke. One guy, a particular good friend of mine, he had $100 ,000. That's a lot of money for a common plug. He had no education, couldn't read or write. And he got buying cars and Cadillacs and Elkins and all that. And drinking and driving. So one day he run into a doctorate in town in his car and he went, I think the doctor died killing. Well that cost him a lot of money to get out of that. Then he got in trouble with income tax. Income tax people and they got on tell and they broke him. He died, he didn't have no more than I got.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Was that around Bovill or was that in Spokane?

speaker: Michael Bubuly
words: No, that's the guy that he used to be. He used to work in the woods. He worked in the woods a lot but he got into the business down here. That's the way he got away. I believe if I ever got into that much money, I don't think I'd stay in the business. I think I'd quit the business and I'll just take it to life easy. I'd take a little bit of traveling. I'd go here and there. If I see a place that I'd stay at the rest of the day to stop for a few days, I'll stop there and keep going. I think I could spend a lot of time that way. But he didn't see Charlie Aaron left out of town. I guess you'd get greedy. I guess you'd get greedy. He didn't seem to be greedy. He's free with his money. He spent. But money was coming in. He wasn't stingy. He wasn't stingy to hell. Somebody needed him. He'd hand him the money. Somebody hungry. He'd feed them and see that they had a place to stay and stuff like that. He just figured that's going to keep coming all the time. Once you get in trouble, once Uncle Sam gets after you, you've got to have a lot of money to fight here.

speaker: Michael Bubuly
words: No, nothing, sir.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: No more?

speaker: Michael Bubuly
words: No, he's just one of two days enough to me. I don't have any idea. It's probably not interesting. No, it is interesting. It is.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: What about Pat?

speaker: Michael Bubuly
words: Pat Malone.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Was he really deaf on bootleggers or not?

speaker: Michael Bubuly
words: He's a guy in drinks.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Why was he deaf?

speaker: Michael Bubuly
words: Not really unless they got out of Moscow, unless they sure really got out of them. He had his own initiative. I don't think he would bother very much. But he had to do something in there. People from town complained, why? Of course. He got his orders from Moscow and he had to do something. And the old man, he was getting old, and there was nothing else he could do. So he had to do something. He had to make a little showing. I don't think he was really bad, he really wanted to hurt anybody or really be dirty with him on his own. But he had to have something to do. He made a little showing. I don't think that's why. But one thing about Pat, he always warned you. He said, how warned you? Just as much as he said, what for me?

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Were there people in Bovill or a group in Bovill that put pressure on Pat and gave him a hard time?

speaker: Michael Bubuly
words: Yeah, a lot of the lawyers. I had nothing against the churches. A lot of those church women. I had nothing against the church, but the church should stay with its own scope. They don't believe in drinking, which they have not right, but I don't think they should try to control somebody else's life. If you want a drink, if you want to do things, well it's none of my business. You're still a human being to me and a friend. Regardless of what you do, if you don't try to hurt me, do me harm. Some people are different because they don't believe in something that you shouldn't be doing, what they don't want to do. We're all individuals and we have our own way of seeing things or interpreting things. And old Pat, he was a good guy. We had listened to people. Of course, naturally people was paying him, and he was getting paid from the taxpayer money, and he had to do something for us. But I don't think he was not too unreasonable. I think he was a good old guy.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: When did you first hear about the Wobblies in the woods?

speaker: Michael Bubuly
words: Oh, it was shortly after I came to the country. I think it was about 1915 or somewhere along there. They showed up around there. IWW. Of course, there were even existence long before that. In different places. But the Potlatch was always kind of tough against the unionism of any kind. They didn't want men to organize me or anything. But I'm all for unions. Unions are all right. The union has got the system, but the Wobblies didn't have no system. They had no system whatsoever. They had no leaders. There was no leaders there. Somebody like myself comes in and you join, it only cost you two and a half or something. You join and they give you a little card. I worried about two and a half went. Nobody knows, because there was no headquarters. There were no locals. I couldn't see going in or anything like that. I belonged to the union. I belonged to Peeves, Tharjir, and the International Labor Organization and United Mine Workers. Yeah, union is all right. For working people, it wasn't for that. I don't think working people would get very far. It would be improved as much as they are now if it hadn't been organized as labor. But I couldn't see it in IWW. I couldn't get their system through my head.

speaker: Michael Bubuly
words: How are they going to have an organization, no treasury, no organized headquarters to handle things? They didn't have a man in each camp that was permanent, that was the head that was the representative? No, no, no. They couldn't let him stay in camp. The guy would come along and see you in town or sometimes they would come and sneak in through camp and try to sell some memberships and tell you, explain things or something like that. That was probably a good idea, but there was no system to it. You take the United Mine Workers, you join there and you'll write on the books and you pay your dues and they'll go to bat for you. And their wages is negotiated through your leaders. They had the bylaws and agreements that unions, the cat break, or the corporation cat break. They are broken occasionally, but they went into trouble when they did. But IWW didn't have that. You have nobody to negotiate with the corporation because you haven't got no authority. You've got to have authority from some place in order to come in and represent a bunch of men. They couldn't show that to nobody because that organization was not recognized, not even by labor or the government. And therefore I didn't take it to term. There was right set up. They meant well, but then what they meant is what they could do with different things.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: So you enjoyed them?

speaker: Michael Bubuly
words: No, I didn't. I never did enjoy them. When they had a lot of trouble there around Moscow, they had a stockade belt around there, you know, to put those men in there. The guys that went on a strike, the belt was stockade like he grew for stock. And I heard of them in there, put them in there. I wasn't around there then. I went to work, I went to Wyoming, I went to work in the mine. And they came for help for the IWWs, because a lot of people donated the money and sent them. And they helped out when they had a big affair in Centralia. Some of them, I guess it was shooting, killing and everything happened in Centralia. Well, there was a drive to raise funds for the guys that was in jail. Of course, a lot of people donated. In fact, I did too. I paid $2 .00, probably $5 .00 or $10 .00 towards that cause, because they had talked to this anyway. If it wasn't exactly legal, it was for benefit of towards the working people, and I thought probably good idea.

speaker: Michael Bubuly
words: But United Mine Workers, they were one of the best unions that was ever organized. During John L. Lewis, the old labor leader, one that they couldn't find anything wrong with, he was a leader that was incorruptible. He couldn't be corrupted. He talked to President Roosevelt, just the same as he would to me and he talked to the big corporation heads, just as tough as he would to anybody else. And they couldn't say that he was a communist, socialist, or anything else. They couldn't find anything corrupt about him and the labor. But he was only one that I know of in the last 50 years, or 60. The rest of them all gets power drunk, money hungry, corrupt, sell out, sell the labor off for their own benefit. But John L. Lewis was the one that didn't do it. I know they offered to raise his salary from $25 ,000 a year to $50 ,000. The union itself offered to raise his wages to $50 ,000 a year. He said, no, $25 ,000 is not for me. I don't need more. Look, after he died, what Tony Boyle did. He goes to work and then raises his own salary to $50 ,000 a year and sets his own pension for $50 ,000 a year after retirement.

speaker: Michael Bubuly
words: Then one that opposed him, Yablonsky, Jacques Yablonsky, he goes to raise to have him murdered, him and his wife and her daughter, just to better himself to keep that $50 ,000 a year in pensions. I want to ask you about one thing more about the IWW.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Do you think that it was the IWW that got the better conditions in the camps after the war?

speaker: Michael Bubuly
words: Well, undoubtedly, it did. They finally realized, the corporations finally realized that they have to better their conditions or have trouble all the time. So they did. They made better living quarters, better camps, better board needing conditions, and furnished the bedding, which lumberjack in those days had to carry his own bedding, always belonging on his back, from camp to camp, town to camp. They changed that. They did that, I know, that they were the ones that brought that up. That's one good term that I know they did in a labor movement. And if they had a right start, if they had the leader, like John L. Lewis, they would have done a lot better, but they didn't have anyone that I know that was worthy of support to carry on a labor organization. I did call Bill Heywood, tell me he was a communist, that he was the head of the whole thing, and he was back there in Chicago somewhere. He didn't know anything about what's going on out here, and didn't know anything about the conditions. Nobody's ever seen him out here. If he'd have been like John L. Lewis, he'd have been out to see what the conditions are, because evidently he must have been paid by somebody, some of the laborer, mostly from these guys working people.

speaker: Michael Bubuly
words: I understand what he had been in the Western Federation of Miners before he got into the IWW. He had been in the, or yeah, I think that's what it is. Heywood

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Yeah, had been in there. Then he got into the IWW after that. I don't know. I don't remember him ever being in the mining guard. It wasn't mine workers, it was the other union, the Western Federation of Miners. Oh, it could be, it could be, it could be. He's not a united mine worker. He could have been in the Western Federation of Miners or something like that. He had his fingers in a lot of things, but he couldn't have got by in united mine workers, because there was just one head in that. That was John L. Lewis. And before him was Green, William Green.

speaker: Michael Bubuly
words: After John L. Lewis took over, and nobody else was, nobody else was handed out but him. He had these guys under him, you know. Like Tony Boyle, he was under John L. Lewis. He was all right, as long as he, John L. was around. But after John L. passed away, of course, Boyle inherited the presidency, and he really corrupted things when it comes to arranging for murders of things, for his own benefit. That's very serious. And just to get power, that's all. Money hungry, power, you think they're above, well, like our good President Nixon, he thought he was above God and everybody else. That's the way it was with Boyle. He thought nobody could do what he could do, and he'd do what he likes. By the way, the corner with him, and he was too bad, and they didn't make a bit of consequences. Good. He's sick, I feel sorry for anybody that's sick, but a man like that, he didn't feel sorry for him. He didn't feel sorry for the family or the people he had murdered. You know, the people, the guy that, Yablonsky was probably, hell, the better man than Boyle. So, if he'd had went crooked and cheated on votes and all that, then when the other guy was going to expose it all, he had it all to expose it. He hasn't killed doing it. That's not really the way of doing things right.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Well, I'm going to have to get going, I think.

speaker: Michael Bubuly
words: I'm going to have to get going. You have to get going. I forgot to ask you, maybe you'd like to have a cup of coffee or something. I'll tell you, though, listen, I'm going to come back.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: I'd like to come back again this week. In fact, I'll tell you, I'd like to come back twice.
